# Exodus Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~42v–43r (PDF pp. 79–80); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 57–58 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_016_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — PROSE NARRATIVE WITH EMBEDDED DIVINE SPEECH:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 16 in standard prose column format, with no special scribal layout. The chapter is one of the longest in Exodus, containing extensive divine speech (vv. 4–5, 8, 12, 16–18, 23, 25–26, 28–29, 32–33), the first Torah legislation regarding Shabbat (vv. 23–30), and the only instance in the Pentateuch of the phrase מָן הוּא (man hu, "what is it?") as an etymology for manna (v. 15). The Masoretic paragraph markers (setumah/petuchah) divide the chapter into clear units: the murmuring (vv. 1–3), YHWH's provision (vv. 4–12), the manna described (vv. 13–21), the Shabbat (vv. 22–30), and the memorial jar (vv. 31–36). The LC page 001 contains the opening of the chapter (continuing from chapter 15), and page 002 continues through the end. The Vaticanus text (pp. 57–58) shows the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script with chapter/section markings visible in the margins.

---

## The Murmuring of the Congregation (vv. 1–3)

**1** And they journeyed [vayis'u] from Eilim, and all the congregation ['adat] of the sons [benei] of Yisra'el came [vayavo'u] to the midbar [wilderness] of Sin, which [is] between Eilim and between Sinai, on the fifteenth [ba-khamishah asar] day [yom] of the second [ha-sheni] month [la-khodesh] of their going out [le-tsetam] from the land [me-erets] of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסְעוּ מֵאֵילִם וַיָּבֹאוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־מִדְבַּר־סִין אֲשֶׁר בֵּין־אֵילִם וּבֵין סִינַי בַּחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי לְצֵאתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayis'u me-Eilim vayavo'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el el-midbar-Sin asher bein-Eilim u-vein Sinai ba-khamishah asar yom la-khodesh ha-sheni le-tsetam me-erets Mitsrayim, "and they journeyed from Eilim, and all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el came to the wilderness of Sin, which [is] between Eilim and between Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month of their going out from the land of Mitsrayim"). **Key terms:** עֲדַת (adat) = "congregation of" — construct of עֵדָה (edah, "congregation, assembly, community"), from יעד (ya'ad, "to appoint, meet by appointment"). This is the formal assembly of Yisra'el as a gathered body. מִדְבַּר סִין (midbar Sin) = "wilderness of Sin" — not to be confused with סִינַי (Sinai). The name סִין (Sin) is of uncertain etymology, possibly related to the Akkadian name for the moon-god, or to סְנֶה (seneh, "thornbush"). The precise chronological marker — the fifteenth day of the second month — places this event exactly one month after the exodus (the departure from Egypt was on the fifteenth of the first month, cf. Num 33:3). One month of freedom, and the food has run out. LXX: καὶ ἀπῆραν ἐξ Αιλιμ, καὶ ἤλθοσαν πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ εἰς τὴν ἔρημον Σιν, ὅ ἐστιν ἀνὰ μέσον Αιλιμ καὶ ἀνὰ μέσον Σινα· τῇ πεντεκαιδεκάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τῷ μηνὶ τῷ δευτέρῳ ἐξεληλυθότων αὐτῶν ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("and they departed from Ailim, and all the synagogue of the sons of Israel came into the wilderness of Sin, which is between Ailim and between Sina, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their having gone out from the land of Egypt") — συναγωγή ("synagogue, gathering, assembly") for edah.

**2** And all the congregation [kol-adat] of the sons of Yisra'el grumbled [vayillinu] against Mosheh and against Aharon in the midbar [wilderness].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּלּוֹנוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל עַל־מֹשֶׁה וְעַל־אַהֲרֹן בַּמִּדְבָּר (vayillonu kol-adat benei-Yisra'el al-Mosheh ve-al-Aharon ba-midbar, "and all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el grumbled against Mosheh and against Aharon in the wilderness"). **Key terms:** וַיִּלּוֹנוּ (vayillonu) = niphal of לון (lun, "to murmur, grumble") — the same verb from 15:24 at Marah. The complaint now escalates: at Marah it was "the people" (ha-am); here it is "all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el" (kol-adat benei-Yisra'el) — the entire formal assembly. And the complaint is now directed against both Mosheh and Aharon (at Marah, only Mosheh). The wilderness (midbar) is the setting for Israel's recurrent pattern: provision, crisis, complaint, divine response. LXX: καὶ διεγόγγυζεν πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων ("and all the synagogue of the sons of Israel murmured against Mosheh and Aaron") — διαγογγύζω ("murmur throughout, grumble") for vayillonu.

**3** And the sons of Yisra'el said to them: "Would that [mi yitten] we had died [muteinu] by the hand [be-yad] of YHWH in the land [be-erets] of Mitsrayim, in our sitting [be-shivteinu] by [al] the pot [sir] of flesh [ha-basar], in our eating [be-okhlenu] lekhem [bread/food] to satisfaction [la-sova] — for you have brought us out [hotsatem] to this midbar [wilderness] to kill [le-hamit] all this assembly [qahal] by famine [ba-ra'av]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵהֶם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִי־יִתֵּן מוּתֵנוּ בְיַד־יְהוָה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּשִׁבְתֵּנוּ עַל־סִיר הַבָּשָׂר בְּאָכְלֵנוּ לֶחֶם לָשֹׂבַע כִּי־הוֹצֵאתֶם אֹתָנוּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר הַזֶּה לְהָמִית אֶת־כָּל־הַקָּהָל הַזֶּה בָּרָעָב (vayomeru aleihem benei Yisra'el mi-yitten muteinu ve-yad-YHWH be-erets Mitsrayim be-shivteinu al-sir ha-basar be-okhlenu lekhem la-sova ki-hotsatem otanu el-ha-midbar ha-zeh le-hamit et-kol-ha-qahal ha-zeh ba-ra'av, "and the sons of Yisra'el said to them: 'Would that we had died by the hand of YHWH in the land of Mitsrayim, in our sitting by the pot of flesh, in our eating bread to satisfaction — for you have brought us out to this wilderness to kill all this assembly by famine'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the nostalgia of slavery:** The expression מִי־יִתֵּן (mi-yitten) = literally "who will give?" — an idiom expressing an impossible wish: "would that! / if only!" The infinitive construct מוּתֵנוּ (muteinu) = "our dying" — from מות (mut, "to die") + 1cp suffix. The people wish they had died in Egypt rather than face starvation in freedom. This is the first occurrence of the "death-wish" motif that recurs throughout the wilderness narrative (cf. Num 11:15; 14:2; 20:3). The romanticized memory of Egypt is striking: סִיר הַבָּשָׂר (sir ha-basar) = "the pot of flesh/meat" — from סִיר (sir, "pot, cooking pot") + בָּשָׂר (basar, "flesh, meat"). Slaves remembering Egypt as a land of abundant food. לֶחֶם לָשֹׂבַע (lekhem la-sova) = "bread/food to satisfaction/fullness" — from לֶחֶם (lekhem, "bread, food") + שֹׂבַע (sova, "satiety, fullness," from שׂבע, sava, "to be sated, satisfied"). The word קָהָל (qahal) = "assembly, congregation" — a near-synonym of עֵדָה (edah) but emphasizing the gathered crowd. רָעָב (ra'av) = "famine, hunger" — from רעב (ra'ev, "to be hungry"). The accusation is that Mosheh and Aharon have led them out to commit mass murder by starvation. LXX: καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτοὺς οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ· ὄφελον ἀπεθάνομεν πληγέντες ὑπὸ κυρίου ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ, ὅταν ἐκαθίσαμεν ἐπὶ τῶν λεβήτων τῶν κρεῶν καὶ ἠσθίομεν ἄρτους εἰς πλησμονήν· ὅτι ἐξηγάγετε ἡμᾶς εἰς τὴν ἔρημον ταύτην ἀποκτεῖναι πᾶσαν τὴν συναγωγὴν ταύτην ἐν λιμῷ ("and the sons of Israel said to them: 'Would that we had died, struck by the Lord in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the pots of meat and ate bread to fullness; for you have brought us out into this wilderness to kill all this synagogue by famine'") — πληγέντες ὑπὸ κυρίου ("struck by the Lord") expands be-yad YHWH; λέβης ("cauldron, kettle") for sir; πλησμονή ("fullness, satiety") for sova; λιμός ("famine") for ra'av.

---

## YHWH Promises Bread from Heaven (vv. 4–12)

**4** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Behold me [hineni] raining [mamtir] for you lekhem [bread] from the shamayim [heavens]; and the people shall go out [ve-yatsa] and gather [ve-laqetu] the matter of a day [devar-yom] in its day [be-yomo], so that [lema'an] I may test them [anassenu] — will they walk [ha-yelekh] in my torah [instruction] or not [im-lo]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הִנְנִי מַמְטִיר לָכֶם לֶחֶם מִן־הַשָּׁמָיִם וְיָצָא הָעָם וְלָקְטוּ דְּבַר־יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ לְמַעַן אֲנַסֶּנּוּ הֲיֵלֵךְ בְּתוֹרָתִי אִם־לֹא (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh hineni mamtir lakhem lekhem min-ha-shamayim ve-yatsa ha-am ve-laqetu devar-yom be-yomo lema'an anassenu ha-yelekh be-torati im-lo, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Behold me raining for you bread from the heavens; and the people shall go out and gather the matter of a day in its day, so that I may test them — will they walk in my torah or not'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — lekhem min-ha-shamayim and the torah test:** The phrase הִנְנִי מַמְטִיר (hineni mamtir) = "behold me raining" — from מטר (matar, "to rain") in the hiphil participle. YHWH Himself is the subject: "I am about to rain." The bread (lekhem) comes מִן־הַשָּׁמָיִם (min-ha-shamayim) = "from the heavens" — divine provision from the sky, bread that has no earthly origin. The phrase דְּבַר־יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ (devar-yom be-yomo) = "the matter/portion of a day in its day" — literally "the word of a day on its day," meaning the daily ration. No hoarding, no surplus — daily dependence on YHWH. The word תּוֹרָה (torah) = "instruction, law, teaching" — from ירה (yarah, "to cast, throw, instruct, teach"); the same root as vayorehu in 15:25. The manna is explicitly framed as a test (נסה, nasah, "to test") of whether Yisra'el will walk (הלך, halakh) in YHWH's torah. The daily gathering is itself torah-observance: trusting God one day at a time. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ὕω ὑμῖν ἄρτους ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ, καὶ ἐξελεύσεται ὁ λαὸς καὶ συλλέξουσιν τὸ τῆς ἡμέρας εἰς ἡμέραν, ὅπως πειράσω αὐτοὺς εἰ πορεύσονται τῷ νόμῳ μου ἢ οὔ ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'Behold, I rain for you bread from heaven, and the people shall go out and gather the [portion] of the day for [each] day, so that I may test them whether they will walk in my law or not'") — ὕω ("rain") for mamtir; νόμος ("law") for torah; πειράζω ("test") for anassenu.

**5** "And it shall be on the sixth [ha-shishi] day [ba-yom], and they shall prepare [ve-hekhinu] what [et-asher] they bring in [yavi'u], and it shall be double [mishneh] over [al] what [asher] they gather [yilqetu] day [by] day [yom yom]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה בַּיּוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי וְהֵכִינוּ אֵת אֲשֶׁר־יָבִיאוּ וְהָיָה מִשְׁנֶה עַל אֲשֶׁר־יִלְקְטוּ יוֹם יוֹם (ve-hayah ba-yom ha-shishi ve-hekhinu et asher-yavi'u ve-hayah mishneh al asher-yilqetu yom yom, "and it shall be on the sixth day, and they shall prepare what they bring in, and it shall be double over what they gather day [by] day"). **Key terms:** הַשִּׁשִּׁי (ha-shishi) = "the sixth" — the ordinal number, pointing forward to the Shabbat legislation in vv. 22–30. The sixth day is the day of preparation. וְהֵכִינוּ (ve-hekhinu) = hiphil perfect (with waw-consecutive) of כון (kun, "to establish, prepare, make ready") — "they shall prepare." מִשְׁנֶה (mishneh) = "double, second, copy" — from שׁנה (shanah, "to repeat, do again"); hence "a double portion." On the sixth day, the manna will be twice the normal amount. This anticipates the Shabbat rest: no gathering on the seventh day, so a double portion on the sixth. יוֹם יוֹם (yom yom) = "day [by] day" — the repetition indicates daily regularity. LXX: καὶ ἔσται τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ καὶ ἑτοιμάσουσιν ὃ ἐὰν εἰσενέγκωσιν, καὶ ἔσται διπλοῦν ὃ ἐὰν συναγάγωσιν τὸ καθ᾽ ἡμέραν ("and it shall be on the sixth day, and they shall prepare whatever they bring in, and it shall be double whatever they gather daily") — διπλοῦς ("double, twofold") for mishneh.

**6** And Mosheh and Aharon said to all the sons of Yisra'el: "[At] evening [erev] you shall know [vi-da'tem] that YHWH brought you out [hotsi] from the land of Mitsrayim;

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־כָּל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל עֶרֶב וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי יְהוָה הוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayomer Mosheh ve-Aharon el-kol-benei Yisra'el erev vi-da'tem ki YHWH hotsi etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim, "and Mosheh and Aharon said to all the sons of Yisra'el: '[At] evening you shall know that YHWH brought you out from the land of Mitsrayim'"). **Key terms:** עֶרֶב (erev) = "evening, dusk" — the time of day when the quail will come (v. 13). The knowledge (יָדַע, yada, "to know") will be experiential — they will know YHWH's deliverance through the tangible arrival of food. הוֹצִיא (hotsi) = hiphil of יצא (yatsa, "to go out") — "He brought out." LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων πρὸς πᾶσαν συναγωγὴν υἱῶν Ισραηλ· ἑσπέρας γνώσεσθε ὅτι κύριος ἐξήγαγεν ὑμᾶς ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("and Mosheh and Aaron said to all the synagogue of the sons of Israel: 'In the evening you shall know that the Lord brought you out from the land of Egypt'") — ἑσπέρα ("evening") for erev.

**7** "and [in the] morning [u-voqer] you shall see [u-re'item] the kavod [glory] of YHWH, in His hearing [be-shom'o] your grumblings [et-telunnoteikhem] against YHWH — and what [are] we [u-mah nakhnu] that you grumble [tallinu] against us?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבֹקֶר וּרְאִיתֶם אֶת־כְּבוֹד יְהוָה בְּשָׁמְעוֹ אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם עַל־יְהוָה וְנַחְנוּ מָה כִּי תַלִּינוּ עָלֵינוּ (u-voqer u-re'item et-kevod YHWH be-shom'o et-telunnoteikhem al-YHWH ve-nakhnu mah ki tallinu aleinu, "and [in the] morning you shall see the glory of YHWH, in His hearing your grumblings against YHWH — and we, what [are we] that you grumble against us?"). **Key terms:** כְּבוֹד (kavod) = "glory, weight, heaviness, honor" — from כבד (kaved, "to be heavy, weighty"). The kavod of YHWH is the manifest, visible, weighty presence of God — His glory made perceptible. In the morning, they will see (ראה, ra'ah) this glory — the manna itself is a manifestation of divine kavod. תְּלֻנֹּת (telunnot) = "grumblings, murmurings" — from לון (lun, "to grumble"); the noun form of the verb used in v. 2. The rhetorical question וְנַחְנוּ מָה (ve-nakhnu mah) = "and we, what [are we]?" — Mosheh and Aharon deflect: the grumbling is not against them but against YHWH. They are merely instruments. LXX: καὶ πρωὶ ὄψεσθε τὴν δόξαν κυρίου ἐν τῷ εἰσακοῦσαι τὸν γογγυσμὸν ὑμῶν ἐπὶ τῷ θεῷ· ἡμεῖς δὲ τί ἐσμεν ὅτι διαγογγύζετε καθ᾽ ἡμῶν; ("and in the morning you shall see the glory of the Lord, when He hears your murmuring against God; and what are we that you murmur against us?") — δόξα ("glory") for kavod; γογγυσμός ("murmuring") for telunnot.

**8** And Mosheh said: "[This will be] in YHWH's giving [be-tet] to you in the evening [ba-erev] flesh [basar] to eat [le-ekhol], and lekhem [bread] in the morning [ba-boqer] to be satisfied [lisbo'a] — in YHWH's hearing [bi-shmo'a] your grumblings [et-telunnoteikhem] which you grumble [asher-atem mallinim] against Him — and what [are] we? Not against us [are] your grumblings [telunnoteikhem], but [ki] against YHWH."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה בְּתֵת יְהוָה לָכֶם בָּעֶרֶב בָּשָׂר לֶאֱכֹל וְלֶחֶם בַּבֹּקֶר לִשְׂבֹּעַ בִּשְׁמֹעַ יְהוָה אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר־אַתֶּם מַלִּינִם עָלָיו וְנַחְנוּ מָה לֹא עָלֵינוּ תְלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם כִּי עַל־יְהוָה (vayomer Mosheh be-tet YHWH lakhem ba-erev basar le-ekhol ve-lekhem ba-boqer lisbo'a bi-shmo'a YHWH et-telunnoteikhem asher-atem mallinim alav ve-nakhnu mah lo aleinu telunnoteikhem ki al-YHWH, "and Mosheh said: 'In YHWH's giving to you in the evening flesh to eat, and bread in the morning to be satisfied — in YHWH's hearing your grumblings which you grumble against Him — and we, what [are we]? Not against us [are] your grumblings, but against YHWH'"). **Key terms:** בְּתֵת (be-tet) = "in the giving of" — infinitive construct of נתן (natan, "to give") with preposition. בָּשָׂר (basar) = "flesh, meat" — the quail of the evening. לֶחֶם (lekhem) = "bread, food" — the manna of the morning. The structure evening-flesh / morning-bread corresponds to the dual provision: quail at dusk, manna at dawn. מַלִּינִם (mallinim) = hiphil participle of לון (lun, "to grumble") — "ones causing to grumble / grumbling." Mosheh insists: the grumbling is ultimately directed at YHWH, not at human leaders. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς· ἐν τῷ διδόναι κύριον ὑμῖν ἑσπέρας κρέα φαγεῖν καὶ ἄρτους τὸ πρωὶ εἰς πλησμονήν, ἐν τῷ εἰσακοῦσαι κύριον τὸν γογγυσμὸν ὑμῶν ὃν ὑμεῖς διαγογγύζετε καθ᾽ ἡμῶν· ἡμεῖς δὲ τί ἐσμεν; οὐ γὰρ καθ᾽ ἡμῶν ὁ γογγυσμὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν ἀλλ᾽ ἢ κατὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("and Mosheh said: 'In the Lord's giving you in the evening meat to eat and bread in the morning to fullness, when the Lord hears your murmuring which you murmur against us — and what are we? For your murmuring is not against us but against God'").

**9** And Mosheh said to Aharon: "Say [emor] to all the congregation [adat] of the sons of Yisra'el: 'Draw near [qirvu] before [lifnei] YHWH, for He has heard [shama] your grumblings [telunnoteikhem].'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן אֱמֹר אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל קִרְבוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה כִּי שָׁמַע אֵת תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם (vayomer Mosheh el-Aharon emor el-kol-adat benei Yisra'el qirvu lifnei YHWH ki shama et telunnoteikhem, "and Mosheh said to Aharon: 'Say to all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el: Draw near before YHWH, for He has heard your grumblings'"). **Key terms:** קִרְבוּ (qirvu) = qal imperative of קרב (qarav, "to draw near, approach") — "draw near!" The command to approach YHWH's presence. The verb qarav is the root of קָרְבָּן (qorban, "offering, sacrifice") — to draw near to YHWH is the fundamental act of worship. שָׁמַע (shama) = "He has heard" — qal perfect of שׁמע. YHWH's hearing is not neutral; it is the prelude to action. He has heard the grumbling and will respond. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς Ααρων· εἰπὸν πάσῃ συναγωγῇ υἱῶν Ισραηλ· προσέλθατε ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ· εἰσακήκοεν γὰρ ὑμῶν τὸν γογγυσμόν ("and Mosheh said to Aaron: 'Say to all the synagogue of the sons of Israel: Come near before God, for He has heard your murmuring'") — προσέρχομαι ("come near, approach") for qirvu.

**10** And it was [vayehi] as Aharon spoke to all the congregation [adat] of the sons of Yisra'el, and they turned [vayifnu] toward the midbar [wilderness], and behold [ve-hinneh] the kavod [glory] of YHWH appeared [nir'ah] in the cloud [be-anan].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי כְּדַבֵּר אַהֲרֹן אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּפְנוּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר וְהִנֵּה כְּבוֹד יְהוָה נִרְאָה בֶּעָנָן (vayehi ke-dabber Aharon el-kol-adat benei-Yisra'el vayifnu el-ha-midbar ve-hinneh kevod YHWH nir'ah be-anan, "and it was as Aharon spoke to all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el, and they turned toward the wilderness, and behold the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the kavod in the anan:** The word עָנָן (anan) = "cloud" — the theophanic cloud that led Yisra'el from Egypt (13:21–22) and stood between them and the Egyptians (14:19–20). The כְּבוֹד יְהוָה (kevod YHWH, "glory of YHWH") is the visible manifestation of the divine presence within the cloud. The verb נִרְאָה (nir'ah) = niphal of ראה (ra'ah, "to see") — "appeared, was seen, became visible." The people turn (פנה, panah, "to turn") toward the wilderness, and there in the cloud they see YHWH's kavod — His weighty, luminous presence. This is the first explicit appearance of the kavod in Exodus (it will become central in chapters 24, 33–34, and 40). LXX: καὶ ἡνίκα ἐλάλει Ααρων πάσῃ συναγωγῇ υἱῶν Ισραηλ, καὶ ἐπεστράφησαν εἰς τὴν ἔρημον, καὶ ἡ δόξα κυρίου ὤφθη ἐν νεφέλῃ ("and when Aaron spoke to all the synagogue of the sons of Israel, and they turned toward the wilderness, and the glory of the Lord appeared in a cloud") — νεφέλη ("cloud") for anan.

**11** And YHWH spoke [va-yedabber] to Mosheh, saying [lemor]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor, "and YHWH spoke to Mosheh, saying"). The standard divine-speech introduction formula.

**12** "I have heard [shamati] the grumblings [telunnot] of the sons of Yisra'el. Speak [dabber] to them, saying: 'Between the evenings [bein ha-arbayim] you shall eat flesh [basar], and in the morning [u-va-boqer] you shall be satisfied [tisbe'u] with lekhem [bread]; and you shall know [vi-da'tem] that I [am] YHWH your Elohim [God].'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שָׁמַעְתִּי אֶת־תְּלוּנֹּת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל דַּבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם לֵאמֹר בֵּין הָעַרְבַּיִם תֹּאכְלוּ בָשָׂר וּבַבֹּקֶר תִּשְׂבְּעוּ־לָחֶם וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם (shamati et-telunnot benei Yisra'el dabber aleihem lemor bein ha-arbayim tokhlu vasar u-va-boqer tisbe'u-lakhem vi-da'tem ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem, "I have heard the grumblings of the sons of Yisra'el. Speak to them, saying: 'Between the evenings you shall eat flesh, and in the morning you shall be satisfied with bread; and you shall know that I [am] YHWH your God'"). **Key terms:** בֵּין הָעַרְבַּיִם (bein ha-arbayim) = "between the evenings" — literally "between the two evenings," a technical expression for the twilight period between afternoon and nightfall (also used for the time of the Pesakh sacrifice, 12:6). The dual form עַרְבַּיִם (arbayim) is debated: either (a) between sunset and full darkness, or (b) between the sun's decline and sunset. The phrase וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה (vi-da'tem ki ani YHWH) = "and you shall know that I am YHWH" — the recognition formula, fundamental to Exodus theology (cf. 6:7; 7:5, 17; 8:22; 10:2; 14:4, 18). Knowledge of YHWH comes through His acts of provision. ## Quail and Manna Arrive (vv. 13–21)

**13** And it was [vayehi] in the evening [ba-erev], and the selav [quail] came up [vatta'al] and covered [vattekas] the camp [et-ha-makhaneh]; and in the morning [u-va-boqer] there was [hayetah] a layer [shikhvat] of dew [ha-tal] around [saviv] the camp [la-makhaneh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בָעֶרֶב וַתַּעַל הַשְּׂלָו וַתְּכַס אֶת־הַמַּחֲנֶה וּבַבֹּקֶר הָיְתָה שִׁכְבַת הַטַּל סָבִיב לַמַּחֲנֶה (vayehi va-erev vatta'al ha-selav vattekas et-ha-makhaneh u-va-boqer hayetah shikhvat ha-tal saviv la-makhaneh, "and it was in the evening, and the quail came up and covered the camp; and in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp"). **Key terms:** שְׂלָו (selav) = "quail" — the common quail (Coturnix coturnix), which migrates in enormous flocks across the Sinai region, often arriving exhausted and landing in great numbers. The word occurs only here, Numbers 11:31–32, and Psalm 105:40. The quail arrives at evening (erev), fulfilling the promise of v. 12. שִׁכְבַת (shikhvat) = "a layer of, a lying of" — from שׁכב (shakhav, "to lie down"); the construct form indicates something spread out, lying flat. טַל (tal) = "dew" — the moisture that forms overnight in desert environments. The manna appears beneath or within this dew layer (cf. Num 11:9). מַחֲנֶה (makhaneh) = "camp, encampment" — from חנה (khanah, "to encamp"). LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο ἑσπέρα, καὶ ἀνέβη ὀρτυγομήτρα καὶ ἐκάλυψεν τὴν παρεμβολήν· τὸ πρωὶ ἐγένετο καταπαυομένης τῆς δρόσου κύκλῳ τῆς παρεμβολῆς ("and it was evening, and the quail-mother came up and covered the camp; in the morning there came to be, as the dew ceased, around the camp") — ὀρτυγομήτρα ("mother-quail, quail") for selav; δρόσος ("dew") for tal; παρεμβολή ("camp") for makhaneh.

**14** And the layer [shikhvat] of dew [ha-tal] went up [vatta'al], and behold [ve-hinneh], upon the face [al-penei] of the midbar [wilderness] — a thin [daq] flaky thing [mekhuspas], thin [daq] like frost [ka-kefor] upon the ground [al-ha-arets].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַתַּעַל שִׁכְבַת הַטָּל וְהִנֵּה עַל־פְּנֵי הַמִּדְבָּר דַּק מְחֻסְפָּס דַּק כַּכְּפֹר עַל־הָאָרֶץ (vatta'al shikhvat ha-tal ve-hinneh al-penei ha-midbar daq mekhuspas daq ka-kefor al-ha-arets, "and the layer of dew went up, and behold, upon the face of the wilderness — a thin, flaky thing, thin like frost upon the ground"). **Key terms:** דַּק (daq) = "thin, fine, small" — from דקק (daqaq, "to be fine, be pulverized, be crushed"). The repetition of daq (twice) emphasizes the extreme fineness of the substance. מְחֻסְפָּס (mekhuspas) = "flaky, scale-like" — a hapax legomenon (occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible). The root is uncertain, possibly related to חספס (khasifas), suggesting something peeled off, flaked, or scaled. The LXX and later Jewish tradition struggled with this rare word. כְּפֹר (kefor) = "frost, hoar-frost" — from כפר (kafar, "to cover"); frost that covers the ground like a white coating. The manna's appearance: thin, flaky, white, covering the desert floor like frost. LXX: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐπὶ πρόσωπον τῆς ἐρήμου λεπτὸν ὡσεὶ κόριον λευκὸν ὡσεὶ πάγος ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ("and behold, upon the face of the wilderness, a thin thing like coriander, white like ice upon the ground") — λεπτόν ("thin, fine") for daq; κόριον ("coriander") is introduced here (cf. v. 31 where the Hebrew itself mentions coriander); πάγος ("frost, ice") for kefor. The LXX adds λευκόν ("white"), anticipating the color description in v. 31.

**15** And the sons of Yisra'el saw [vayir'u], and they said [vayomeru] each man [ish] to his brother [el-akhiv]: "Man hu [What is it?]" — for they did not know [lo yade'u] what [mah] it [was] [hu]. And Mosheh said to them: "It [hu] [is] the lekhem [bread] which YHWH has given [natan] to you to eat [le-okhlah]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּרְאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֹּאמְרוּ אִישׁ אֶל־אָחִיו מָן הוּא כִּי לֹא יָדְעוּ מַה־הוּא וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֲלֵהֶם הוּא הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר נָתַן יְהוָה לָכֶם לְאָכְלָה (vayir'u venei-Yisra'el vayomeru ish el-akhiv man hu ki lo yade'u mah-hu vayomer Mosheh aleihem hu ha-lekhem asher natan YHWH lakhem le-okhlah, "and the sons of Yisra'el saw, and they said each to his brother: 'Man hu!' — for they did not know what it was. And Mosheh said to them: 'It is the bread which YHWH has given to you to eat'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — man hu, the etymology of manna:** The expression מָן הוּא (man hu) is the key phrase of the chapter. The word מָן (man) is either (a) an archaic/dialectal Hebrew interrogative meaning "what?" (related to מָה, mah, "what?"), yielding "What is it?"; or (b) an Egyptian or other loanword. The traditional understanding is that the name מָן (man/manna) derives from this question of bewilderment: "What is this?" — the food is so unprecedented that it has no name, only a question. The etymology is folk-etymological: the food is called "What-is-it?" because that was the people's first response. The verb יָדְעוּ (yade'u) = qal perfect of ידע (yada, "to know") — "they knew." They literally did not know what they were seeing. Mosheh's answer is definitive: הוּא הַלֶּחֶם (hu ha-lekhem) = "it is the bread" — the lekhem min-ha-shamayim promised in v. 4. LXX: ἰδόντες δὲ αὐτὸ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ εἶπαν ἕτερος τῷ ἑτέρῳ· τί ἐστιν τοῦτο; οὐ γὰρ ᾔδεισαν τί ἦν. εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς αὐτούς· οὗτος ὁ ἄρτος ὃν ἔδωκεν κύριος ὑμῖν φαγεῖν ("and the sons of Israel, seeing it, said one to another: 'What is this?' — for they did not know what it was. And Mosheh said to them: 'This is the bread which the Lord has given you to eat'") — τί ἐστιν τοῦτο ("what is this?") for man hu — the LXX translates the Hebraism into straightforward Greek.

**16** "This [is] the thing [ha-davar] which YHWH has commanded [tsivvah]: 'Gather [liqtu] from it [mimmennu] each man [ish] according to [lefi] his eating [okhlo] — an omer [the omer] per head [la-gulgolet], [according to the] number [mispar] of your souls [nafshotekhem]; each man [ish] for those who [la-asher] [are] in his tent [be-oholo] you shall take [tiqqakhu].'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה לִקְטוּ מִמֶּנּוּ אִישׁ לְפִי אָכְלוֹ עֹמֶר לַגֻּלְגֹּלֶת מִסְפַּר נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אִישׁ לַאֲשֶׁר בְּאָהֳלוֹ תִּקָּחוּ (zeh ha-davar asher tsivvah YHWH liqtu mimmennu ish lefi okhlo omer la-gulgolet mispar nafshotekhem ish la-asher be-oholo tiqqakhu, "this [is] the word which YHWH commanded: 'Gather from it each man according to his eating — an omer per head, the number of your souls; each man for those who [are] in his tent you shall take'"). **Key terms:** עֹמֶר (omer) = a dry measure of volume, approximately 2.3 liters or about 2 quarts. Defined in v. 36 as one-tenth of an ephah. The omer is used only in this chapter in the entire Hebrew Bible as a unit of measurement. לַגֻּלְגֹּלֶת (la-gulgolet) = "per head, per skull" — from גֻּלְגֹּלֶת (gulgolet, "skull, head"); used as a counting term meaning "per capita" (cf. Num 1:2, 18, 20, 22). The word is the origin of "Golgotha" in the NT (the place of the skull). נַפְשֹׁת (nafshot) = plural of נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "soul, person, living being"). אֹהֶל (ohel) = "tent" — the family dwelling unit. The instruction is precise: one omer per person, enough for each household. LXX: τοῦτο τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ συνέταξεν κύριος· συναγάγετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἕκαστος εἰς τοὺς καθήκοντας, γομορ κατὰ κεφαλήν, κατὰ ἀριθμὸν ψυχῶν ὑμῶν, ἕκαστος σὺν τοῖς συσκηνίοις ὑμῶν συλλέξατε ("this [is] the word which the Lord commanded: gather from it, each to those fitting, a gomor per head, according to the number of your souls; each with your tent-companions, collect") — γομορ (gomor, transliteration) for omer; κεφαλή ("head") for gulgolet.

**17** And the sons of Yisra'el did so [vaya'asu khen], and they gathered [vayilqetu], the one making much [ha-marbeh] and the one making little [ve-ha-mam'it].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּלְקְטוּ הַמַּרְבֶּה וְהַמַּמְעִיט (vaya'asu khen benei Yisra'el vayilqetu ha-marbeh ve-ha-mam'it, "and the sons of Yisra'el did so, and they gathered, the one making much and the one making little"). **Key terms:** הַמַּרְבֶּה (ha-marbeh) = "the one making much / the one who gathered much" — hiphil participle of רבה (ravah, "to be many, increase"). הַמַּמְעִיט (ha-mam'it) = "the one making little / the one who gathered little" — hiphil participle of מעט (ma'at, "to be few, be small"). Some gathered more, some less — the contrast sets up the miracle described in the next verse. LXX: καὶ ἐποίησαν οὕτως οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ, καὶ συνέλεξαν ὁ τὸ πολὺ καὶ ὁ τὸ ἔλαττον ("and the sons of Israel did so, and they gathered — the one much and the one less").

**18** And they measured [vayamoddu] by the omer [ba-omer], and the one making much [ha-marbeh] did not have excess [lo he'edif], and the one making little [ha-mam'it] did not lack [lo hakhsir]; each man [ish] according to [lefi] his eating [okhlo] they gathered [laqatu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּמֹדּוּ בָעֹמֶר וְלֹא הֶעְדִּיף הַמַּרְבֶּה וְהַמַּמְעִיט לֹא הֶחְסִיר אִישׁ לְפִי־אָכְלוֹ לָקָטוּ (vayamoddu va-omer ve-lo he'edif ha-marbeh ve-ha-mam'it lo hakhsir ish lefi-okhlo laqatu, "and they measured by the omer, and the one making much did not have excess, and the one making little did not lack; each man according to his eating they gathered"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the miracle of exact sufficiency:** The verb הֶעְדִּיף (he'edif) = hiphil of עדף (adaf, "to be in excess, have a surplus") — "he did not have excess/surplus." הֶחְסִיר (hakhsir) = hiphil of חסר (khasar, "to lack, be in want, decrease") — "he did not lack." This is a remarkable miracle: regardless of how much each person gathered, when measured, everyone had exactly one omer — no more, no less. The manna defies the economics of accumulation; it enforces equality. Paul cites this verse in 2 Corinthians 8:15 as a principle of community sharing. LXX: καὶ μετρήσαντες τῷ γομορ οὐκ ἐπλεόνασεν ὁ τὸ πολύ, καὶ ὁ τὸ ἔλαττον οὐκ ἠλαττόνησεν· ἕκαστος εἰς τοὺς καθήκοντας παρ᾽ αὐτῷ συνέλεξαν ("and measuring with the gomor, the one [gathering] much did not have more, and the one [gathering] less did not have less; each to those fitting with him they gathered") — πλεονάζω ("have excess") for he'edif; ἐλαττονέω ("have less") for hakhsir.

**19** And Mosheh said to them: "Let no man [ish] leave over [al-yoter] from it [mimmennu] until morning [ad-boqer]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֲלֵהֶם אִישׁ אַל־יוֹתֵר מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר (vayomer Mosheh aleihem ish al-yoter mimmennu ad-boqer, "and Mosheh said to them: 'Let no man leave over from it until morning'"). **Key terms:** אַל־יוֹתֵר (al-yoter) = "let not [anyone] leave over / leave remaining" — from יתר (yatar, "to remain over, be left over"); the hiphil with the negative particle אַל (al, jussive negation). The command prohibits hoarding. The manna must be consumed the same day it is gathered — it is daily bread, not a commodity to be stored. This enforces radical daily dependence on YHWH. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς αὐτούς· μηδεὶς καταλιπέτω ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸ πρωί ("and Mosheh said to them: 'Let no one leave of it until morning'") — καταλείπω ("leave behind") for yoter.

**20** And they did not listen [ve-lo shame'u] to Mosheh, and men [anashim] left over [vayotiru] from it [mimmennu] until morning [ad-boqer], and it bred [vayarum] worms [tola'im], and it stank [vayiv'ash]; and Mosheh was angry [vayiqtsof] with them.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְלֹא־שָׁמְעוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיּוֹתִרוּ אֲנָשִׁים מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר וַיָּרֻם תּוֹלָעִים וַיִּבְאַשׁ וַיִּקְצֹף עֲלֵהֶם מֹשֶׁה (ve-lo-shame'u el-Mosheh vayotiru anashim mimmennu ad-boqer vayarum tola'im vayiv'ash vayiqtsof aleihem Mosheh, "and they did not listen to Mosheh, and men left over from it until morning, and it bred worms, and it stank; and Mosheh was angry with them"). **Key terms:** וַיּוֹתִרוּ (vayotiru) = hiphil of יתר (yatar, "to leave over") — "they caused to remain, they left over." The disobedience is immediate and concrete. תּוֹלָעִים (tola'im) = "worms, maggots" — plural of תּוֹלָע (tola, "worm, crimson-worm"). The hoarded manna rots overnight — it breeds maggots and stinks. The divine provision cannot be stockpiled; it spoils when hoarded. וַיִּבְאַשׁ (vayiv'ash) = qal of באשׁ (ba'ash, "to stink, become foul") — "it stank." וַיִּקְצֹף (vayiqtsof) = qal of קצף (qatsaf, "to be angry, be wrathful") — "he was angry." Mosheh's anger reflects YHWH's displeasure at disobedience. **21** And they gathered it [vayilqetu] morning by morning [ba-boqer ba-boqer], each man [ish] according to [lefi] his eating [okhlo]; and when the sun [ha-shemesh] grew hot [ve-kham], it melted [ve-namas].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּלְקְטוּ אֹתוֹ בַּבֹּקֶר בַּבֹּקֶר אִישׁ לְפִי אָכְלוֹ וְחַם הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְנָמָס (vayilqetu oto ba-boqer ba-boqer ish lefi okhlo ve-kham ha-shemesh ve-namas, "and they gathered it morning by morning, each man according to his eating; and the sun grew hot, and it melted"). **Key terms:** בַּבֹּקֶר בַּבֹּקֶר (ba-boqer ba-boqer) = "in the morning, in the morning" — the repetition indicates daily regularity, every single morning. ## The Shabbat — First Torah Legislation (vv. 22–30)

**22** And it was [vayehi] on the sixth [ha-shishi] day [ba-yom], they gathered [laqetu] double [mishneh] lekhem [bread/food] — two omers [shenei ha-omer] for the one [la-ekhad]; and all the leaders [nesi'ei] of the congregation [ha-edah] came [vayavo'u] and they told [vayaggidu] Mosheh.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי לָקְטוּ לֶחֶם מִשְׁנֶה שְׁנֵי הָעֹמֶר לָאֶחָד וַיָּבֹאוּ כָּל־נְשִׂיאֵי הָעֵדָה וַיַּגִּידוּ לְמֹשֶׁה (vayehi ba-yom ha-shishi laqetu lekhem mishneh shenei ha-omer la-ekhad vayavo'u kol-nesi'ei ha-edah vayaggidu le-Mosheh, "and it was on the sixth day, they gathered double bread — two omers for the one; and all the leaders of the congregation came and told Mosheh"). **Key terms:** מִשְׁנֶה (mishneh) = "double" — as in v. 5, the double portion appears on the sixth day as YHWH foretold. שְׁנֵי הָעֹמֶר (shenei ha-omer) = "two of the omer" = two omers per person, confirming the double amount. נְשִׂיאֵי (nesi'ei) = "leaders, princes, chiefs" — plural construct of נָשִׂיא (nasi, "prince, leader, one lifted up"), from נשׂא (nasa, "to lift up, carry, bear"). The leaders report to Mosheh — they are surprised by the double quantity and seek explanation. LXX: ἐγένετο δὲ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἕκτῃ συνέλεξαν τὰ δέοντα διπλᾶ, δύο γομορ τῷ ἑνί· εἰσήλθοσαν δὲ πάντες οἱ ἄρχοντες τῆς συναγωγῆς καὶ ἀνήγγειλαν Μωυσῇ ("and it was on the sixth day, they gathered the needful things double, two gomor for each one; and all the rulers of the synagogue came in and reported to Mosheh") — ἄρχων ("ruler") for nasi.

**23** And he said to them: "It [is] what [hu asher] YHWH spoke [dibber]. A Shabbaton [solemn rest] — a Shabbat [sabbath] of qodesh [holiness] to YHWH [is] tomorrow [makhar]. What [et-asher] you would bake [tofu], bake [efu]; and what [ve-et-asher] you would boil [tevashelu], boil [bashelu]; and all the excess [ha-odef], set aside [hannikhu] for you for keeping [le-mishmeret] until the morning [ad-ha-boqer]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם הוּא אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה שַׁבָּתוֹן שַׁבַּת־קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה מָחָר אֵת אֲשֶׁר־תֹּאפוּ אֵפוּ וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר־תְּבַשְּׁלוּ בַּשֵּׁלוּ וְאֵת כָּל־הָעֹדֵף הַנִּיחוּ לָכֶם לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת עַד־הַבֹּקֶר (vayomer aleihem hu asher dibber YHWH shabbaton shabbat-qodesh la-YHWH makhar et asher-tofu efu ve-et asher-tevashelu bashelu ve-et kol-ha-odef hannikhu lakhem le-mishmeret ad-ha-boqer, "and he said to them: 'It [is] what YHWH spoke. A solemn rest — a holy sabbath to YHWH [is] tomorrow. What you would bake, bake; and what you would boil, boil; and all the excess, set aside for you for keeping until the morning'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the first Shabbat legislation in Torah:** This is the first time the word שַׁבָּת (Shabbat) appears in the Torah as a divine institution (the creation narrative in Gen 2:2–3 uses the verb שָׁבַת, shavat, "he rested/ceased," but does not call it "Shabbat" or legislate its observance). שַׁבָּתוֹן (shabbaton) = "solemn rest, complete rest" — an intensive form of Shabbat, emphasizing the fullness of the cessation. שַׁבַּת קֹדֶשׁ (shabbat qodesh) = "a sabbath of holiness" = "a holy sabbath." The combination shabbaton + shabbat qodesh doubles the emphasis. תֹּאפוּ...אֵפוּ (tofu...efu) = "you would bake...bake!" — from אפה (afah, "to bake"). תְּבַשְּׁלוּ...בַּשֵּׁלוּ (tevashelu...bashelu) = "you would boil/cook...boil/cook!" — from בשׁל (bashal, "to boil, cook"). All cooking must be done on the sixth day; no cooking on Shabbat. הָעֹדֵף (ha-odef) = "the excess, the surplus, what is left over" — from עדף (adaf, "to be in excess"). לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת (le-mishmeret) = "for keeping, for safeguarding" — from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard"). On this day alone, the leftover manna will not spoil. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· τοῦτο τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐλάλησεν κύριος· ἀνάπαυσις ἁγία σαββάτων τῷ κυρίῳ αὔριον· ὅσα ἐὰν πέσσητε, πέσσετε, καὶ ὅσα ἐὰν ἕψητε, ἕψετε· καὶ πᾶν τὸ πλεονάζον καταλίπετε αὐτὸ εἰς ἀποθήκην εἰς τὸ πρωί ("and he said to them: 'This is the word which the Lord spoke: a holy rest of sabbaths to the Lord [is] tomorrow. Whatever you would bake, bake, and whatever you would boil, boil; and all the excess, leave it in storage until morning'") — ἀνάπαυσις ("rest, repose") for shabbaton; σάββατα ("sabbaths") for shabbat.

**24** And they set it aside [vayyannikhu] until the morning [ad-ha-boqer], as Mosheh commanded [tsivvah]; and it did not stink [ve-lo hiv'ish], and no worm [ve-rimmah] was in it [lo hayetah bo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּנִּיחוּ אֹתוֹ עַד־הַבֹּקֶר כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה מֹשֶׁה וְלֹא הִבְאִישׁ וְרִמָּה לֹא־הָיְתָה בּוֹ (vayyannikhu oto ad-ha-boqer ka-asher tsivvah Mosheh ve-lo hiv'ish ve-rimmah lo-hayetah bo, "and they set it aside until the morning, as Mosheh commanded; and it did not stink, and no worm was in it"). **Key terms:** וַיַּנִּיחוּ (vayyannikhu) = hiphil of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, settle") — "they set down, they placed." הִבְאִישׁ (hiv'ish) = hiphil of באשׁ (ba'ash, "to stink") — "it did not become foul." Contrast with v. 20 where the hoarded manna bred worms and stank. On the sixth day, the preserved manna remains fresh — a Shabbat miracle. רִמָּה (rimmah) = "worm, maggot" — a different word from תּוֹלָעִים (tola'im) in v. 20, but synonymous. The double negative (did not stink + no worms) emphasizes the complete preservation. LXX: καὶ κατελίποσαν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸ πρωὶ καθάπερ συνέταξεν αὐτοῖς Μωυσῆς· καὶ οὐκ ἐπώζεσεν, οὐδὲ σκώληξ ἐγένετο ἐν αὐτῷ ("and they left of it until morning just as Mosheh commanded them; and it did not stink, nor did a worm come to be in it").

**25** And Mosheh said: "Eat it [ikhluhu] today [ha-yom], for today [is] a Shabbat to YHWH; today you will not find it [lo timtsa'uhu] in the field [ba-sadeh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אִכְלֻהוּ הַיּוֹם כִּי־שַׁבָּת הַיּוֹם לַיהוָה הַיּוֹם לֹא תִמְצָאֻהוּ בַּשָּׂדֶה (vayomer Mosheh ikhluhu ha-yom ki-Shabbat ha-yom la-YHWH ha-yom lo timtsa'uhu ba-sadeh, "and Mosheh said: 'Eat it today, for today [is] a Shabbat to YHWH; today you will not find it in the field'"). **Key terms:** The threefold repetition of הַיּוֹם (ha-yom, "today") — eat it today / a Shabbat today / today you will not find it — hammers the point: Shabbat is this day, this present moment of rest. שָׂדֶה (sadeh) = "field, open country" — the area outside the camp where the manna falls. On Shabbat, the field will be empty. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς· φάγετε σήμερον· ἔστιν γὰρ σάββατα σήμερον τῷ κυρίῳ· οὐχ εὑρεθήσεται ἐν τῷ πεδίῳ ("and Mosheh said: 'Eat today, for today [is] a sabbath to the Lord; it will not be found in the plain'") — πεδίον ("plain, field") for sadeh.

**26** Six [sheshet] days [yamim] you shall gather it [tilqetuhu], and on the seventh [ha-shevi'i] day [u-va-yom] — Shabbat — it shall not be [lo yihyeh] in it [bo]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים תִּלְקְטֻהוּ וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שַׁבָּת לֹא יִהְיֶה־בּוֹ (sheshet yamim tilqetuhu u-va-yom ha-shevi'i Shabbat lo yihyeh-bo, "six days you shall gather it, and on the seventh day — Shabbat — it shall not be in it"). **Key terms:** שֵׁשֶׁת (sheshet) = "six of" — construct form of שֵׁשׁ (shesh, "six"). The six/seven pattern echoes the creation week (Gen 1:1–2:3). הַשְּׁבִיעִי (ha-shevi'i) = "the seventh" — from שֶׁבַע (sheva, "seven"). The Shabbat rhythm is built into the manna itself: six days it falls, the seventh it does not. Creation order governs provision. The disobedience is understated but clear: despite Mosheh's explicit instruction (vv. 25–26), some went out anyway. They found nothing — the manna's absence on Shabbat is itself a divine teaching tool. LXX: ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ ἐξήλθοσάν τινες ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ συλλέξαι καὶ οὐχ εὗρον ("and it was on the seventh day, some of the people went out to gather and did not find").

**28** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "How long [ad-anah] do you refuse [me'antem] to keep [lishmor] my commandments [mitsvotai] and my torot [laws/instructions]?

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה עַד־אָנָה מֵאַנְתֶּם לִשְׁמֹר מִצְוֹתַי וְתוֹרֹתָי (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ad-anah me'antem lishmor mitsvotai ve-torotai, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'How long do you refuse to keep my commandments and my laws?'"). **Key terms:** עַד־אָנָה (ad-anah) = "until when? how long?" — an expression of divine exasperation (cf. ## Manna Described and the Memorial Jar (vv. 31–36)

**31** And the house [beit] of Yisra'el called [vayiqre'u] its name [shemo] man [manna]; and it [was] like seed [ke-zera] of coriander [gad], white [lavan], and its taste [ve-ta'mo] [was] like a wafer [ke-tsappikheit] with honey [bi-dvash].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקְרְאוּ בֵית־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־שְׁמוֹ מָן וְהוּא כְּזֶרַע גַּד לָבָן וְטַעְמוֹ כְּצַפִּיחִת בִּדְבָשׁ (vayiqre'u veit-Yisra'el et-shemo man ve-hu ke-zera gad lavan ve-ta'mo ke-tsappikheit bi-dvash, "and the house of Yisra'el called its name 'man'; and it [was] like the seed of coriander, white, and its taste [was] like a wafer with honey"). **Key terms:** מָן (man) = "manna" — the name is now fixed, derived from the question of v. 15. בֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל (beit Yisra'el) = "the house of Yisra'el" — the people as a household/family unit. זֶרַע גַּד (zera gad) = "seed of coriander" — from זֶרַע (zera, "seed") + גַּד (gad, "coriander," Coriandrum sativum). Coriander seeds are small, round, and pale. In Numbers 11:7, the manna is compared to בְּדֹלַח (bedolakh, "bdellium"), a translucent resin. לָבָן (lavan) = "white" — the color of the manna. טַעַם (ta'am) = "taste, flavor" — from טעם (ta'am, "to taste, perceive"). צַפִּיחִת (tsappikheit) = "wafer, flat cake" — a thin, flat pastry. This word occurs only here and in the parallel at Numbers 11:8. דְּבַשׁ (devash) = "honey" — either bee-honey or date-honey (fruit syrup). The manna tastes like a sweet, flat wafer made with honey. LXX: καὶ ἐπωνόμασαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ μαν· ἦν δὲ ὡς σπέρμα κορίου λευκόν, τὸ δὲ γεῦμα αὐτοῦ ὡς ἐγκρὶς ἐν μέλιτι ("and the sons of Israel named its name 'man'; and it was like white coriander seed, and its taste [was] like a cake in honey") — κόριον ("coriander") for gad; ἐγκρίς ("cake, honey-cake") for tsappikheit; μέλι ("honey") for devash.

**32** And Mosheh said: "This [is] the thing [ha-davar] which YHWH commanded [tsivvah]: 'A fullness of the omer [melo ha-omer] from it [mimmennu] for keeping [le-mishmeret] for your generations [le-doroteikhem], so that [lema'an] they may see [yir'u] the lekhem [bread] which I fed you [he'ekhalti etkhem] in the midbar [wilderness] in my bringing you out [be-hotsi'i etkhem] from the land [me-erets] of Mitsrayim.'"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה מְלֹא הָעֹמֶר מִמֶּנּוּ לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם לְמַעַן יִרְאוּ אֶת־הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר הֶאֱכַלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם בַּמִּדְבָּר בְּהוֹצִיאִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayomer Mosheh zeh ha-davar asher tsivvah YHWH melo ha-omer mimmennu le-mishmeret le-doroteikhem lema'an yir'u et-ha-lekhem asher he'ekhalti etkhem ba-midbar be-hotsi'i etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim, "and Mosheh said: 'This [is] the word which YHWH commanded: A fullness of the omer from it for keeping for your generations, so that they may see the bread which I fed you in the wilderness in my bringing you out from the land of Mitsrayim'"). **Key terms:** מְלֹא (melo) = "fullness of, the full amount of" — from מלא (male, "to be full"). The full omer is to be preserved as a memorial. לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (le-doroteikhem) = "for your generations" — from דּוֹר (dor, "generation"). The manna is not only provision for the present but a teaching aid for the future. הֶאֱכַלְתִּי (he'ekhalti) = hiphil of אכל (akhal, "to eat") — "I caused to eat / I fed." YHWH speaks in the first person: "I fed you." The memorial jar will be physical evidence for later generations of how YHWH sustained Yisra'el in the wilderness. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς· τοῦτο τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ συνέταξεν κύριος· πλήσατε τὸ γομορ τοῦ μαν εἰς ἀποθήκην εἰς τὰς γενεὰς ὑμῶν, ἵνα ἴδωσιν τὸν ἄρτον ὃν ἐφάγετε ὑμεῖς ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ, ὡς ἐξήγαγεν ὑμᾶς κύριος ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("and Mosheh said: 'This is the word which the Lord commanded: fill the gomor of man for storage for your generations, that they may see the bread which you ate in the wilderness, when the Lord brought you out from the land of Egypt'") — the LXX shifts from "I fed you" (first person divine speech) to "you ate" (second person narrative), a notable theological softening.

**33** And Mosheh said to Aharon: "Take [qakh] one [ekhad] tsintseneth [jar] and put [ve-ten] there [shammah] a fullness of the omer [melo-ha-omer] of man [manna], and set it aside [ve-hannakh] before [lifnei] YHWH for keeping [le-mishmeret] for your generations [le-doroteikhem]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן קַח צִנְצֶנֶת אַחַת וְתֶן־שָׁמָּה מְלֹא־הָעֹמֶר מָן וְהַנַּח אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (vayomer Mosheh el-Aharon qakh tsintseneth akhat ve-ten-shammah melo-ha-omer man ve-hannakh oto lifnei YHWH le-mishmeret le-doroteikhem, "and Mosheh said to Aharon: 'Take one jar and put there a fullness of the omer of manna, and set it aside before YHWH for keeping for your generations'"). **Key terms:** צִנְצֶנֶת (tsintseneth) = "jar, pot, vessel" — a hapax legomenon (occurs only here and in v. 34). The word's etymology is uncertain; some connect it to an Egyptian loanword. The LXX renders it στάμνος (stamnos, "jar, urn"). In Hebrews 9:4, this jar is described as χρυσῆ στάμνος (khruse stamnos, "golden jar"), though the Hebrew text does not specify the material. לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei YHWH) = "before YHWH" — this phrase points to the sacred space where YHWH's presence dwells. The jar is to be placed "before YHWH" — later understood as within the Ark of the Testimony (cf. v. 34 and Heb 9:4). LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς πρὸς Ααρων· λαβὲ στάμνον χρυσοῦν ἕνα καὶ ἔμβαλε εἰς αὐτὸν πλῆρες τὸ γομορ τοῦ μαν καὶ ἀποθήσεις αὐτὸ ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ εἰς διατήρησιν εἰς τὰς γενεὰς ὑμῶν ("and Mosheh said to Aaron: 'Take one golden jar and put into it the full gomor of man, and you shall store it before God for preservation for your generations'") — **LXX ADDITION: χρυσοῦν (khrysoun, "golden")** — the LXX adds "golden," which is not in the Hebrew. This interpretive addition later influences Hebrews 9:4.

**34** As [ka-asher] YHWH commanded [tsivvah] Mosheh, Aharon set it aside [vayyannikhehu] before [lifnei] the edut [testimony] for keeping [le-mishmaret].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיַּנִּיחֵהוּ אַהֲרֹן לִפְנֵי הָעֵדֻת לְמִשְׁמָרֶת (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH el-Mosheh vayyannikhehu Aharon lifnei ha-edut le-mishmaret, "as YHWH commanded Mosheh, Aharon set it aside before the testimony for keeping"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — ha-edut (the testimony):** The word הָעֵדֻת (ha-edut) = "the testimony, the witness" — from עוד (ud, "to testify, bear witness"). This term refers to the stone tablets of the covenant (the "tablets of the testimony," lukhot ha-edut, 31:18; 32:15; 34:29) and by extension to the Ark of the Testimony (aron ha-edut, 25:22) in which they are kept. The manna jar is placed "before the edut" — before the Ark, in the Tabernacle. This is a proleptic (anticipatory) reference: the Ark and Tabernacle have not yet been built at this point in the narrative (they are constructed in chs. 25–40). The narrator writes from the vantage point of the completed sanctuary. LXX: ὃν τρόπον συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ, καὶ ἀπέθετο Ααρων ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ εἰς διατήρησιν ("in the manner the Lord commanded Mosheh, Aaron stored it before God for preservation") — the LXX renders lifnei ha-edut ("before the testimony") as ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before God"), avoiding the specific reference to the Ark/testimony tablets.

**35** And the sons of Yisra'el ate [akhlu] the man [manna] forty [arba'im] years [shanah], until their coming [ad-bo'am] to a land [el-erets] inhabited [noshavet]; the man [manna] they ate [akhlu] until their coming [ad-bo'am] to the border [el-qetseh] of the land [erets] of Kena'an [Canaan].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אָכְלוּ אֶת־הַמָּן אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה עַד־בֹּאָם אֶל־אֶרֶץ נוֹשָׁבֶת אֶת־הַמָּן אָכְלוּ עַד־בֹּאָם אֶל־קְצֵה אֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן (u-venei Yisra'el akhlu et-ha-man arba'im shanah ad-bo'am el-erets noshavet et-ha-man akhlu ad-bo'am el-qetseh erets Kena'an, "and the sons of Yisra'el ate the manna forty years, until their coming to an inhabited land; the manna they ate until their coming to the border of the land of Canaan"). **Key terms:** אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה (arba'im shanah) = "forty years" — the duration of the wilderness wandering (cf. Num 14:33; Deut 8:2; 29:5; Josh 5:6). The manna lasted the entire period from Sinai to Canaan. נוֹשָׁבֶת (noshavet) = "inhabited, settled" — niphal participle of ישׁב (yashav, "to dwell, inhabit"). The manna ceases when they reach settled, productive land where they can grow their own food. קְצֵה (qetseh) = "end, edge, border" — from קצה (qatsah, "to cut off"). The repetition (ate the manna...the manna they ate) emphasizes the completeness of YHWH's provision through the entire wilderness period. According to Joshua 5:12, the manna ceased the day after they ate the produce of the land of Canaan. This verse is an editorial note written from the perspective of the settlement. LXX: οἱ δὲ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἔφαγον τὸ μαν ἔτη τεσσεράκοντα, ἕως ἦλθον εἰς γῆν οἰκουμένην· τὸ μαν ἐφάγοσαν ἕως παρεγένοντο εἰς μέρος τῆς Φοινίκης ("and the sons of Israel ate the man forty years, until they came to inhabited land; the man they ate until they arrived at a part of Phoenicia") — Φοινίκη ("Phoenicia") for erets Kena'an — the LXX uses the Greek name for the coastal Canaanite territory.

**36** And the omer [ve-ha-omer] — a tenth [asirit] of the ephah [ha-efah] it [is] [hu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָעֹמֶר עֲשִׂרִית הָאֵיפָה הוּא (ve-ha-omer asirit ha-efah hu, "and the omer — a tenth of the ephah it [is]"). **Key terms:** עֲשִׂרִית (asirit) = "a tenth" — from עֶשֶׂר (eser, "ten"). אֵיפָה (efah) = a dry measure of volume, approximately 23 liters or about 6 gallons. The efah is one of the standard measures of the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ruth 2:17; Isa 5:10; Ezek 45:11). Thus the omer = approximately 2.3 liters. This final verse is an editorial gloss — a note for later readers who may not know the omer measurement, since it is used nowhere else in the Torah as a unit of measure. The efah, by contrast, remained in common use. LXX: τὸ δὲ γομορ τὸ δέκατον τῶν τριῶν μέτρων ἦν ("and the gomor was a tenth of the three measures") — the LXX renders efah as τρία μέτρα ("three measures"), an approximate Greek equivalent.

## Theological Summary

Exodus 16 is the foundational narrative of YHWH's provision in the wilderness and the institution of the Shabbat as a lived, weekly practice. The chapter moves from crisis (hunger) through complaint to divine response, establishing patterns that will define the entire forty-year wilderness period.

Key theological threads:

- **Lekhem min-ha-shamayim (bread from heaven):** The manna is divine provision with no earthly source — it rains from the heavens (v. 4), appears with the dew (v. 14), and melts in the sun (v. 21). It is utterly dependent on YHWH's daily act. Later tradition (Deut 8:3) interprets the manna as teaching that "man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of YHWH."
- **Man hu — the unnamed food:** The manna's very name encodes bewilderment: "What is it?" (v. 15). It is sui generis, a food without category, forcing the people to receive what they cannot comprehend. Faith begins in not-knowing.
- **Daily dependence and the prohibition of hoarding:** The manna must be gathered each morning and consumed the same day (vv. 19–20). Hoarding produces rot and worms. The daily omer enforces radical trust — there is no way to secure tomorrow's provision today (except on the sixth day, by divine command).
- **The miracle of exact sufficiency (v. 18):** Those who gathered much had no surplus; those who gathered little had no lack. The manna equalizes — it is provision calibrated precisely to need, not to effort or greed.
- **The Shabbat as gift (vv. 22–30):** The Shabbat emerges here not as arbitrary prohibition but as divine gift (v. 29: "YHWH has given you the Shabbat"). The double portion on the sixth day proves that rest is built into the fabric of creation and provision. The first Shabbat law in Torah is embedded in food, not in cult.
- **The kavod of YHWH in the anan (v. 10):** The divine glory appears in the cloud — YHWH's response to the murmuring is not punishment but self-revelation. The kavod is the visible, weighty presence of the God who feeds.
- **The memorial tsintseneth (vv. 32–34):** An omer of manna is preserved "before the edut" — before the Ark of the Testimony — as a perpetual witness for future generations. Food becomes monument; provision becomes testimony.
- **Forty years (v. 35):** The manna sustains Yisra'el for the entire wilderness period — from Sinai to Canaan, from exodus to settlement. YHWH's provision is not a one-time miracle but a sustained, daily commitment spanning a generation.
